text:
Royal Free Hospital
Department of Neurology

Clinic Date: 14 September 2025

Dr A. Mercer
Consultant Neurologist
Royal Free Hospital
Department of Neurology, Pond Street, London NW3 2QG

Dear Dr A. Mercer
Consultant Neurologist,

Daniel Hart, DOB: 17-03-1986, Hospital No: H927451 NHS No. 6073194821
12 Brookfield Road, London, N6 5AN

Thank you for asking me to review this gentleman. I met him in clinic today accompanied by his partner. He reports episodic events going back many years. The typical spell, as described by his partner, begins with a brief disengagement and guttural vocalisation, followed by rhythmic rocking of the trunk while unresponsive; he subsequently appears confused and fatigued, with recovery over approximately 45–60 minutes. He does not recall most of the episode. There have been rare events during sleep where she has noticed similar rocking and a short cry, with him being difficult to rouse afterwards. He also describes separate episodes where he becomes very tearful and overwhelmed without loss of awareness; these were volunteered as distinct from the episodes outlined above.

In terms of timing, they currently cluster such that he tends to experience a spell roughly once in a fortnight—he phrased it as happening about every second week—though there can be longer quiet intervals followed by two close together. He feels this interval has been broadly stable over the past year.

He cycles to work most days and states he prefers this for routine and fitness; he has not noticed any consistent relationship between commuting days and the occurrence of events. Sleep has been variable due to workload, and he occasionally wakes unrefreshed.

Past medications have included levetiracetam (mood irritability), carbamazepine (dizziness), and topiramate (word-finding difficulty). Currently he is taking brivaracetam 100 mg twice daily and lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily, which he reports are generally well tolerated. He has had no drug allergies.

Neurological examination today was unremarkable. Mood appeared tense but coherent, with no thought disorder elicited. There is a prior history of a brief post-event confusional state requiring short observation in 2023, but no recent behavioural disturbance.

Given the longstanding history and the present description, I would like to obtain prolonged video-EEG monitoring with sleep capture to better characterise these events, and I have discussed this with him and his partner. He is agreeable. I have also requested updated MRI brain (3T) with epilepsy-focused protocol. Bloods arranged today include FBC, U&E, LFT, vitamin D, and trough levels for lamotrigine and brivaracetam for reference. We will review results and consider rationalisation of therapy depending on the monitoring outcome.

I will keep you informed of progress. Please let me know if there are any interim concerns from your side.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: he tends to experience a spell roughly once in a fortnight—he phrased it as happening about every second week

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes the current timing: "he tends to experience a spell roughly once in a fortnight—he phrased it as happening about every second week—though there can be longer quiet intervals followed by two close together." This indicates approximately 1 event every 2 weeks. There is mention of rare sleep events and separate non-impairment episodes, but the main seizure-like spells occur about once per two weeks. No evidence of seizure freedom for 6 months. Highest frequency is thus 1 per 2 weeks.
seizure_frequency_number: he tends to experience a spell roughly once in a fortnight—he phrased it as happening about every second week
